Blow-up limit L*=1; rational-phase trig selector; weighted sum mod 1000

Let f be the unique real-analytic function near x=0 satisfying the singular ODE
$$
x\,f'(x)=x+f(x)^2,\qquad f(0)=0,\qquad f'(0)=1.
$$
Let $\beta>0$ be the (finite) right endpoint of the maximal interval of existence of f on $(0,\beta)$, and define
$$
L_\ast=\lim_{x\to \beta^-}\Big(\frac{\beta-x}{\beta}\Big)f(x)
$$
(which exists).

Define
$$
R=\sum_{n=1}^{2025} n\cdot \mathbf{1}\Big(\cos\big(\tfrac{\pi n}{7}+\pi L_\ast\big)>0\ \text{ and }\ \sin\big(\tfrac{\pi n}{9}-\tfrac{\pi}{2}L_\ast\big)<0\Big).
$$
Compute $R \bmod 1000$ (as the representative in $\{0,1,\dots,999\}$).

Solution.
Step 1 (Series uniqueness at the singular point).
Write $f(x)=\sum_{m\ge 0} a_m x^m$ with $a_0=0,\ a_1=1$. Then
$$
x f'(x)=\sum_{m\ge 1} m a_m x^m,\qquad x+f(x)^2 = x + \sum_{m\ge 0}\Big(\sum_{i+j=m} a_i a_j\Big)x^m.
$$
Matching coefficients gives:
- at $x^1$: $a_1=1$ (consistent),
- for $m\ge 2$: $\displaystyle m a_m = \sum_{i=1}^{m-1} a_i a_{m-i}$.
Thus $a_m$ is determined recursively from earlier coefficients, so the real-analytic germ at 0 is unique.

Step 2 (Finite-time blow-up and the normalized blow-up limit $L_\ast=1$).
For $x>0$, the ODE is
$$
f'(x)=1+\frac{f(x)^2}{x}>0,
$$
so $f$ is strictly increasing on $(0,\beta)$. Also, for $x\ge 1$,
$$
f'(x)\ge \frac{f(x)^2}{x}.
On any interval where $f(x)>0$ (true for all sufficiently small $x>0$ since $f'(0)=1$), we can divide by $f(x)^2$:
\frac{f'(x)}{f(x)^2}\ge \frac1x
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\Big(\frac1{f(x)}\Big)'=-\frac{f'(x)}{f(x)^2}\le -\frac1x.
$$
Integrating from $1$ to $x$ gives
$$
\frac1{f(x)} \le \frac1{f(1)}-\ln x.
$$
The right-hand side becomes negative for large $x$, impossible while $f(x)$ is finite and positive. Hence the solution cannot exist for all $x>0$; thus $\beta<\infty$. Moreover, since $f$ is increasing, $\lim_{x\to\beta^-} f(x)$ exists in $\mathbb{R}\cup\{+\infty\}$. If it were finite, the standard ODE continuation theorem (the vector field $1+f^2/x$ is smooth for $x>0$) would extend the solution past $\beta$, contradicting maximality. Therefore
$$
\lim_{x\to\beta^-} f(x)=+\infty.
$$

Now compute $L_\ast$. From the ODE,
$$
\frac{f'(x)}{f(x)^2}=\frac{1}{f(x)^2}+\frac{1}{x}.
$$
As $x\to\beta^-$, we have $x\to\beta$ and $f(x)\to+\infty$, so $\frac{1}{f(x)^2}\to 0$ and hence
$$
\lim_{x\to\beta^-}\frac{f'(x)}{f(x)^2}=\frac{1}{\beta}.
$$
But $\big(\frac{1}{f}\big)'=-\frac{f'}{f^2}$, so asymptotically
$$
\Big(\frac{1}{f(x)}\Big)'\to -\frac{1}{\beta}.
$$
With $\frac{1}{f(x)}\to 0$ as $x\to\beta^-$, this yields
$$
\frac{1}{f(x)}\sim \frac{\beta-x}{\beta}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\Big(\frac{\beta-x}{\beta}\Big)f(x)\to 1.
$$
Therefore $L_\ast=1$.

Step 3 (Reduce the trigonometric selector to congruence conditions).
Substitute $L_\ast=1$:
$$
\cos\Big(\frac{\pi n}{7}+\pi\Big)>0
\iff
-\cos\Big(\frac{\pi n}{7}\Big)>0
\iff
\cos\Big(\frac{\pi n}{7}\Big)<0,
$$
and
$$
\sin\Big(\frac{\pi n}{9}-\frac{\pi}{2}\Big)<0
\iff
-\cos\Big(\frac{\pi n}{9}\Big)<0
\iff
\cos\Big(\frac{\pi n}{9}\Big)>0.
$$
Now:
- $\cos(\pi n/7)$ has period $14$ in $n$. It is negative exactly when $\frac{\pi n}{7}\in(\frac{\pi}{2},\frac{3\pi}{2})$ modulo $2\pi$, i.e. when
  $$
  n \bmod 14 \in \{4,5,6,7,8,9,10\}.
  $$
- $\cos(\pi n/9)$ has period $18$ in $n$. It is positive for angles in $(-\frac{\pi}{2},\frac{\pi}{2})\cup(\frac{3\pi}{2},2\pi)$ modulo $2\pi$, which corresponds to
  $$
  n \bmod 18 \in \{0,1,2,3,4,14,15,16,17\}.
  $$
No equality-to-zero issues occur because $\cos(\pi n/7)=0$ would require $n=7/2+7k$ and $\cos(\pi n/9)=0$ would require $n=9/2+9k$, neither of which is an integer.

Thus the indicator depends only on $n \bmod \mathrm{lcm}(14,18)=126$.

Step 4 (One-period residue set and block summation up to 2025).
Let $S\subset\{1,2,\dots,126\}$ be the set of residues satisfying both congruence conditions. Because $\gcd(14,18)=2$, a pair of congruences $n\equiv r\pmod{14}$, $n\equiv s\pmod{18}$ is solvable iff $r\equiv s\pmod2$. Count residues:
- Among $\{4,5,6,7,8,9,10\}$ there are $4$ even and $3$ odd residues.
- Among $\{0,1,2,3,4,14,15,16,17\}$ there are $5$ even and $4$ odd residues.
So
$$
|S| = 4\cdot 5 + 3\cdot 4 = 32.
Solving the congruences (finite CRT enumeration) yields
S=\{4,18,19,20,21,22,32,33,34,35,36,37,38,50,51,52,74,75,76,88,89,90,91,92,93,94,104,105,106,107,108,122\}.
$$
Group-summing gives
$$
\sum_{r\in S} r
=4+(18+19+20+21+22)+(32+\cdots+38)+(50+51+52)+(74+75+76)+(88+\cdots+94)+(104+\cdots+108)+122
=2016.
$$

Now $2025 = 16\cdot 126 + 9$. Over each full block $\{126j+1,\dots,126j+126\}$, the contributing indices are $\{126j+r:\ r\in S\}$, so the block contribution is
$$
\sum_{r\in S}(126j+r)=126j\cdot |S|+\sum_{r\in S}r
=126j\cdot 32 + 2016.
$$
Summing over $j=0,1,\dots,15$:
$$
R_{\text{full}}=\sum_{j=0}^{15}\big(126j\cdot 32 + 2016\big)
=32\cdot 126\sum_{j=0}^{15} j + 16\cdot 2016
=32\cdot 126\cdot 120 + 32256
=483840+32256
=516096.
$$
In the remaining numbers $2017,\dots,2025$ (i.e. residues $1,\dots,9$ in the next period), only residue $4$ is in $S$, so the only additional contributing term is $2016+4=2020$. Hence
$$
R=516096+2020=518116,
\qquad
R\bmod 1000 = 116.
$$

Answer: $\boxed{116}$.

Uniqueness Test
- (ODE/germ uniqueness) The coefficient recursion $m a_m=\sum_{i=1}^{m-1}a_i a_{m-i}$ for $m\ge2$ with $a_0=0,a_1=1$ determines a unique analytic germ at $0$. Thus any real-analytic solution satisfying $f(0)=0,f'(0)=1$ agrees with this germ.
- (Maximal continuation and $L_\ast$) On $(0,\infty)$, the ODE is smooth away from $x=0$. The comparison argument forces a finite blow-up time $\beta$, and maximality forces $f(x)\to+\infty$ at $\beta$. The limit $L_\ast=\lim_{x\to\beta^-}\big(\frac{\beta-x}{\beta}\big)f(x)$ is uniquely determined by the asymptotic relation $\frac{f'}{f^2}\to\frac1\beta$, giving $L_\ast=1$ uniquely.
- (Discrete selector uniqueness) With $L_\ast=1$, the trigonometric inequalities reduce deterministically to congruence conditions modulo $14$ and $18$, hence modulo $126$. The residue set $S$ is uniquely determined, so the weighted sum $R$ over $n\le 2025$ is uniquely determined. Therefore $R\bmod 1000$ is unique.

116
